---
title: "Chapter 44 — 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 43 · 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference](ch43.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [45 · 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML →](ch45.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Modern machine learning gives us models that predict well but explain nothing: a gradient-boosted ensemble or a billion-parameter transformer is a black box that maps inputs to outputs through millions of opaque numbers. **Explainable AI (XAI)** is the discipline of recovering *why* a model decided what it did — turning that opacity into something a human can inspect, trust, debug, and contest. It sits at the responsibility layer of the AI stack: not about making models more accurate, but about making their behavior legible to the people who build, deploy, and are affected by them.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Production & responsibility layer · used for trust, debugging, regulatory compliance, and fairness auditing · the one key idea: an explanation is a *faithful, human-readable account of why a model produced a specific output* — and faithfulness and readability are often in tension.
:::

## 44.1 — Why explanations matter, and what they actually are

Imagine a bank model denies someone a loan. The applicant has a legal and moral right to ask "why?" — and "the neural network said so" is not an answer. This single scenario surfaces every reason XAI exists.

There are four practical drivers. **Trust**: people adopt a model only if its reasoning looks sensible; a radiologist will not act on a tumor flag they cannot scrutinize. **Debugging**: explanations expose when a model is right for the wrong reason — the classic case is a husky-vs-wolf classifier that secretly keys on snow in the background. **Regulation**: laws like the EU's GDPR and the AI Act grant a qualified "right to an explanation" for automated decisions, and finance (credit) and medicine demand auditable reasoning. **Fairness**: to know whether a model discriminates, you must see *which features drive its decisions* (covered in depth in the ethics chapter).

It helps to be precise about what an explanation *is*. An explanation is a simplified, human-comprehensible surrogate for the model's true computation. Two properties matter and trade off against each other:

- **Faithfulness** (a.k.a. fidelity): how accurately the explanation reflects what the model *actually* computed.
- **Comprehensibility**: how easily a human can understand it.

A perfectly faithful "explanation" of a deep net is the net itself — useless to a human. A simple story a person grasps instantly may be a comforting fiction. Good XAI navigates this tension honestly rather than pretending it does not exist.

::: {.callout-warning}
An explanation that is *plausible* is not necessarily *faithful*. A method can produce a clean, convincing-looking story that has little to do with the model's real reasoning. Always ask: would this explanation change if the model's actual computation changed? If not, it is decoration, not explanation.
:::

## 44.2 — Two axes: intrinsic vs post-hoc, global vs local

Every explanation method sits on two axes, and naming them up front organizes the whole field.

The first axis is **when** interpretability comes from. **Intrinsic interpretability** means the model is transparent by construction — you read the model itself. A linear regression's coefficients, a shallow decision tree's branches, or a short rule list *are* the explanation. **Post-hoc interpretability** means the model is a black box and you attach a separate explanation method after training (LIME, SHAP, saliency maps). Intrinsic is faithful by definition but limits model choice; post-hoc works on any model but only approximates it.

The second axis is **scope**. A **global explanation** describes the model's overall behavior — "across all customers, income is the most important feature." A **local explanation** describes one specific prediction — "*this* customer was denied because their debt-to-income ratio was 0.6." Global answers "how does the model behave in general?"; local answers "why this one decision?". Regulators and affected individuals usually want local; model builders and auditors often want global.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Need to explain a model] --> B{Model already<br/>transparent?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Intrinsic:<br/>read coefficients,<br/>tree, rules]
    B -->|No| D[Post-hoc method]
    D --> E{Scope?}
    E -->|Whole model| F[Global:<br/>perm. importance,<br/>PDP, global SHAP]
    E -->|One prediction| G[Local:<br/>LIME, SHAP,<br/>saliency, counterfactual]
```

The four quadrants are not mutually exclusive — SHAP, for instance, gives local explanations that you can aggregate into a global picture. But knowing which quadrant you need keeps you from reaching for a saliency map when a coefficient table would do.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: reach for the simplest method that answers your actual question. If a linear model is accurate enough, its coefficients beat any post-hoc method on a black box — they are exactly faithful and free.
:::

## 44.3 — Intrinsically interpretable models

The cheapest explanation is the one you do not have to compute: build a model that is readable on its face. Three families dominate.

**Linear and logistic models** explain a prediction as a weighted sum. In logistic regression, $\log\frac{p}{1-p} = \beta_0 + \sum_j \beta_j x_j$, each coefficient $\beta_j$ is the change in log-odds per unit of feature $x_j$, holding others fixed. Exponentiating gives an odds ratio: $e^{\beta_j}=1.3$ means a one-unit increase multiplies the odds by 1.3. This is wonderfully direct — but only if features are on comparable scales and not heavily correlated, since correlation makes individual coefficients unstable and hard to read in isolation.

**Decision trees** explain a prediction as the path from root to leaf: a chain of if-then tests a human can read aloud ("income < 40k → debt > 0.5 → deny"). Trees handle nonlinearity and interactions natively. The catch is depth: a tree of depth 3 is a transparent flowchart, a tree of depth 20 is as inscrutable as any black box. Interpretability is a property of *shallow* trees.

**Rule lists and rule sets** are ordered if-then-else rules, the most natural format for human reasoning. Methods like RIPPER or Bayesian rule lists learn compact rules directly. A related modern family is the **generalized additive model (GAM)** — and its boosted cousin, the **explainable boosting machine (EBM)** — which models the output as a sum of per-feature shape functions $f(x) = \beta_0 + \sum_j f_j(x_j)$. Because each feature contributes an independent, plottable curve, a GAM captures nonlinearity while staying fully readable: you can look at exactly how each feature bends the prediction.

The deeper point: interpretability is not free model capacity. Constraining a model to be readable usually costs some accuracy — the famous **accuracy-interpretability tradeoff** of Section 44.8. But the cost is often smaller than assumed; on tabular data a well-tuned interpretable model frequently matches a black box, and you should always check before paying for opacity you may not need.

## 44.4 — Feature importance: permutation and partial dependence

Once you accept a black box, the first question is usually global: *which features does this model rely on?* Two classic, model-agnostic tools answer it.

**Permutation importance** rests on a beautifully simple idea: if a feature matters, scrambling it should hurt the model. You take a trained model and a held-out set, record the baseline score, then randomly shuffle one feature's column (breaking its link to the target while keeping its marginal distribution), and re-score. The drop in performance is that feature's importance.

$$\text{Imp}(j) = s_{\text{baseline}} - \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} s\big(\text{model}, X^{(\text{perm } j, k)}\big)$$

averaged over $K$ shuffles to reduce noise. It is model-agnostic, cheap, and intuitive.

```python
import numpy as np

def permutation_importance(model, X, y, score_fn, n_repeats=5):
    base = score_fn(y, model.predict(X))   # baseline performance
    imp = np.zeros(X.shape[1])
    for j in range(X.shape[1]):
        drops = []
        for _ in range(n_repeats):
            Xp = X.copy()
            np.random.shuffle(Xp[:, j])     # break feature j's signal
            drops.append(base - score_fn(y, model.predict(Xp)))
        imp[j] = np.mean(drops)             # avg performance lost
    return imp
```

The pitfall: with **correlated features**, permutation importance misleads. If height and weight are correlated and you shuffle height alone, you create unrealistic giants, and importance gets split or understated because the model leans on the correlated twin. Treat it as a guide, not gospel, when features are entangled.

**Partial dependence plots (PDP)** answer a different question: not *how much* a feature matters but *in which direction*. A PDP shows the average predicted output as you sweep one feature across its range, marginalizing over all others: $\text{PD}_j(v) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i f(x_i \text{ with feature } j \text{ set to } v)$. An upward PDP says "more of this feature pushes the prediction up." Its blind spot is the same correlation problem (it averages over feature combinations that may never occur) and it hides heterogeneity — an average curve can be flat while individuals swing wildly in opposite directions. **ICE plots** (Individual Conditional Expectation — one line per instance instead of the average) reveal that hidden spread.

## 44.5 — LIME and SHAP: local attributions done right

The headline post-hoc methods both answer the local question — *why this prediction?* — by attributing the output to individual features, but they get there differently.

**LIME** (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) reasons that a complex decision boundary, *zoomed in around one point*, looks roughly linear. So to explain prediction $f(x)$, LIME samples perturbed points near $x$, gets the black box's prediction for each, weights them by closeness to $x$, and fits a simple interpretable model (usually sparse linear) on that local neighborhood. The simple model's coefficients are the explanation. LIME is flexible (works on text, images, tabular) and intuitive, but fragile: the explanation depends on how you sample and define "near," so two runs can disagree — a real stability concern.

**SHAP** (SHapley Additive exPlanations) puts attribution on a rigorous footing borrowed from cooperative game theory. Treat each feature as a *player* and the prediction as a *payout*. The **Shapley value** is the unique way to fairly divide the payout among players, defined as a feature's average marginal contribution across all orderings in which features could be added:

$$\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq F\setminus\{j\}} \frac{|S|!\,(|F|-|S|-1)!}{|F|!}\big[f(S\cup\{j\}) - f(S)\big]$$

In words: for every coalition $S$ of other features, measure how much adding feature $j$ changes the prediction, then average those marginal gains with the right combinatorial weights. SHAP is the only attribution method satisfying a set of fairness axioms simultaneously — **local accuracy** (attributions sum to the prediction minus a baseline), **missingness**, and **consistency** (if a feature's contribution grows, its attribution never shrinks). That theoretical grounding is why SHAP became the default.

The price is cost: the exact sum is exponential in the number of features. In practice you use approximations — **KernelSHAP** (a sampling/regression scheme, model-agnostic) or **TreeSHAP** (exact and fast for tree ensembles).

| | LIME | SHAP |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | local linear surrogate | Shapley values (game theory) |
| Guarantees | none formal | additivity, consistency, local accuracy |
| Stability | can vary run to run | deterministic (exact); stable |
| Cost | cheap | expensive (exact); TreeSHAP fast for trees |
| Output | local only | local, aggregable to global |

::: {.callout-warning}
SHAP's additive attributions are not causal claims. $\phi_j$ tells you how feature $j$ contributed *to the model's output*, given how the model uses it — not how changing $j$ in the real world would change the outcome. A feature can have a large SHAP value purely because it proxies for something the model latched onto. For real-world "what if I change this" questions, see the causal inference chapter.
:::

## 44.6 — Explaining vision models: saliency and Grad-CAM

For an image classifier, the natural explanation is a heatmap: *which pixels made you say "cat"?* This is the **saliency** family.

The simplest version uses gradients. The model output for the predicted class is a function of the input pixels, so the gradient $\frac{\partial f_c}{\partial x}$ measures how sensitive the score for class $c$ is to each pixel — large magnitude means a small change there swings the prediction. A **vanilla saliency map** is just that gradient, visualized. It is cheap but noisy and tends to scatter; refinements (SmoothGrad, Integrated Gradients) average or integrate gradients to clean it up and add desirable properties.

**Grad-CAM** (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) is the workhorse for CNNs and gives coarser but far more reliable, object-level maps. The intuition: the last convolutional layer's feature maps still carry spatial structure ("where things are") plus high-level semantics ("what things are"). Grad-CAM asks which of those feature maps matter for the target class — by using the gradients flowing into that layer as importance weights — then combines them:

$$L^c_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k\right), \qquad \alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial f_c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$$

where $A^k$ is the $k$-th feature map and $\alpha_k^c$ is its gradient-derived weight. The ReLU keeps only regions that *support* the class. Upsampled to the image, it highlights the region the model used — the dog's face, not the grass behind it. This is exactly the lens that catches the snow-detecting "wolf" classifier.

```python
# Grad-CAM sketch: A = activations of last conv layer [K,H,W],
# grads = dScore_c / dA  (same shape), both captured via hooks.
weights = grads.mean(axis=(1, 2))            # alpha_k: GAP over spatial dims
cam = np.maximum((weights[:, None, None] * A).sum(0), 0)  # ReLU(sum_k a_k * A_k)
cam = cam / (cam.max() + 1e-8)               # normalize to [0,1] for overlay
```

The caveat: saliency tells you *where* the model looked, not *what concept* it extracted or *why*. A bright region marks influential pixels, not a reason. And some saliency methods have failed sanity checks — producing similar maps even when the model's weights are randomized — so prefer methods (like Grad-CAM and Integrated Gradients) that pass those tests.

## 44.7 — Counterfactuals, attention, and peering inside the network

This section gathers three explanation styles that reach beyond feature attribution — toward *actionable*, *mechanistic*, and *cautionary* understanding.

**Counterfactual explanations** answer the question people actually ask: not "why was I denied?" but "what would have to change for a yes?" A counterfactual is the smallest, most realistic change to the input that flips the decision — "your loan would be approved if your income were \$5,000 higher." Formally you search for a point $x'$ close to $x$ that the model classifies differently:

$$x' = \arg\min_{x'} \; \underbrace{d(x, x')}_{\text{stay close}} \quad \text{s.t.}\quad f(x') = \text{desired outcome}$$

Counterfactuals are appealing because they are inherently human (we reason in "what ifs"), require no peek inside the model, and double as **recourse** — concrete advice to the affected person. The design care is realism and actionability: a counterfactual that says "be 20 years younger" is mathematically valid and practically useless, so you constrain changes to features the person can actually act on.

**Attention as explanation** is the tempting shortcut for transformers: attention weights show which input tokens each position "attends to," so why not read them as importance? Because the link is unreliable. Influential research showed attention weights are often *not faithful* — you can frequently find very different attention patterns that yield the same prediction, so high attention does not reliably mean "this token caused the output." Attention is a useful *hint* about information flow, not a trustworthy explanation on its own.

**Mechanistic interpretability and probing** take the most ambitious stance: instead of attributing outputs to inputs, *reverse-engineer the internal computation*. Two complementary tools:

- **Probing** trains a small classifier on a network's hidden activations to test whether some property (part-of-speech, sentiment, a board state) is *linearly decodable* there. If a tiny probe recovers it, the information is present and accessibly encoded. The caveat: a probe shows information is *available*, not that the model *uses* it.
- **Mechanistic interpretability** aims to identify actual *circuits* — interpretable subgraphs of neurons and attention heads that implement a behavior (induction heads that copy repeated patterns, "knowledge neurons" storing facts). Techniques include **activation patching** (swap an activation from one run into another and watch the output move, isolating a component's causal role) and **sparse autoencoders** that decompose tangled activations into more monosemantic features.

This frontier matters most for LLMs, where billions of parameters make every input-output method feel shallow. It is also the hardest and least mature corner of XAI — promising for safety and alignment, but far from a turnkey tool.

## 44.8 — The accuracy-interpretability tradeoff and the limits of explanations

A persistent belief holds that you must choose: accurate *or* interpretable, never both. The reality is more nuanced and more hopeful.

The tradeoff is real where the data is genuinely complex — perception (vision, speech) and language, where deep nets dominate and no shallow model competes. There, opacity buys real accuracy. But on **structured/tabular data**, the gap is frequently small or zero: a well-regularized linear model, a shallow tree, or a carefully constrained model (like an explainable boosting machine / GAM) often matches a black box. So the choice is not forced — measure it. Reach for a black box only when you have *checked* that an interpretable model actually costs you something.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Linear / rules] -->|low capacity<br/>high transparency| B[Shallow trees / GAMs]
    B --> C[Random forests / GBMs]
    C -->|high capacity<br/>low transparency| D[Deep nets / LLMs]
    style A fill:#1b5e20,color:#fff
    style D fill:#7b1f1f,color:#fff
```

It is just as important to know the *limits* of explanation itself, because XAI's worst failure mode is false confidence:

- **Explanations can be unfaithful.** Many post-hoc methods approximate the model; the explanation may look clean yet misrepresent the real computation.
- **Explanations can be gamed.** A model can be deliberately or accidentally built so its explanations look fair while its decisions are not — "fairwashing." An audit that trusts the explanation can be fooled.
- **Explanations can be unstable.** LIME and gradient saliency can swing under tiny input or sampling changes; an explanation that flips is hard to trust.
- **Correlation, not causation.** Almost all attribution explains the *model*, not the *world*. "Feature $j$ drove the prediction" is not "changing $j$ changes the outcome."
- **Explanations can manipulate.** A persuasive explanation can engineer trust the model has not earned — dangerous when the model is wrong.

The honest posture: treat explanations as *evidence to be validated*, not as ground truth. Use multiple methods, check that they agree, run sanity tests (do explanations change when the model is randomized?), and prefer intrinsic interpretability when the accuracy cost is small. An explanation is a tool for human judgment, not a substitute for it.

::: {.callout-tip}
Before trusting any post-hoc explanation, run a sanity check: randomize the model's weights or labels. If the explanation barely changes, it is reflecting the input or the method — not the model — and you should not rely on it.
:::

## 44.x — Key takeaways

- **XAI exists for trust, debugging, regulation, and fairness** — turning a black box into something a human can inspect and contest.
- Every method sits on two axes: **intrinsic vs post-hoc** (read the model vs explain it after) and **global vs local** (whole model vs one prediction).
- **Intrinsic models** (linear, shallow trees, rule lists, GAMs/EBMs) are faithful for free; prefer them when the accuracy cost is small — and *measure* that cost.
- **Permutation importance** and **partial dependence/ICE** give global feature importance and direction, but both mislead under correlated features.
- **LIME** fits a local linear surrogate (flexible but unstable); **SHAP** uses Shapley values for axiomatically fair, consistent attributions (sound but costly; fast for trees via TreeSHAP).
- For vision, **Grad-CAM** gives reliable object-level heatmaps of *where* the model looked; raw gradient saliency is noisier and some variants fail sanity checks.
- **Counterfactuals** give actionable recourse ("change X to flip the decision"); **attention is not a faithful explanation**; **probing and mechanistic interpretability** reverse-engineer internal computation, the hardest XAI frontier (key for LLMs).
- Explanations are **evidence, not ground truth**: they can be unfaithful, unstable, gamed, or merely correlational. Validate them; never let them substitute for human judgment.

## 44.y — See also

- **Chapter 05 — Linear & Logistic Models**: the canonical intrinsically interpretable models and their coefficients.
- **Chapter 08 — Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning**: shallow trees as transparent models, and the ensembles that make post-hoc XAI necessary.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers**: what attention weights actually are, before judging them as explanations.
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models**: the models that motivate probing and mechanistic interpretability.
- **Chapter 45 — Causal Inference & Causal ML**: the difference between explaining a model and explaining the world.
- **Chapter 46 — AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance**: where explanations feed fairness audits, recourse, and regulation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 43 · 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference](ch43.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [45 · 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML →](ch45.html)
